title: Framework Comparison Matrix
description: Side-by-side comparison of 11 major AI governance frameworks across 10 key governance dimensions. Color-coded by strength of coverage: strong (comprehensive/binding requirements), partial (addressed but limited), or not addressed.

eu-ai-act.summary: All AI systems placed on EU market. Extraterritorial — applies to non-EU providers serving EU users. Covers providers, deployers, importers, distributors.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: All AI stakeholders in Malaysia — developers, providers, users. Voluntary adoption across all sectors. No extraterritorial reach.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Technology-neutral, applicable to any AI system by any organization. Voluntary — no mandated scope. Referenced in US federal procurement.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: All AI actors across the lifecycle in 46 adopting countries. Broad scope but non-binding — influences national legislation.

singapore-maigf.summary: Organizations deploying AI commercially in Singapore. Sector-agnostic. Agentic AI addendum (2025) extends to autonomous agents.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: All 193 member states' AI policies. Broadest geographic scope of any framework but non-binding.

china-genai.summary: Generative AI services provided to the public in mainland PRC. Covers text, image, audio, video generation. Extraterritorial reach via Art. 20. Binding regulation.

uk-ai-framework.summary: All AI systems in the UK, applied through existing sector-specific regulators. Decentralized — no single AI regulator. Initially non-statutory.

us-ai-policy.summary: Federal government AI use and policy. EO 14179 focuses on innovation and competitiveness. Revoked prior safety-focused EO 14110. Limited to federal scope.

canada-ai.summary: Advanced generative AI systems developed or deployed in Canada. Voluntary Code of Conduct only — AIDA legislation died in Parliament. No binding scope.

iso-42001.summary: Any organization worldwide seeking to establish an AI management system. Voluntary certification standard. Technology and sector neutral.

eu-ai-act.summary: 4-tier system: prohibited, high-risk (Annex III), limited risk, minimal risk. Plus GPAI with systemic risk tier. Most prescriptive classification globally.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: No risk classification tiers. Principles-based approach without differentiating AI systems by risk level.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Context-dependent risk assessment through MAP and MEASURE functions. No fixed tiers — organizations define risk criteria per deployment context.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: No formal risk classification. Recommends risk-proportionate governance but does not define categories.

singapore-maigf.summary: Risk-proportionate approach recommended but no fixed tiers. Organizations self-assess based on probability and severity of harm.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: No risk classification. Values-based rather than risk-based approach to AI governance.

china-genai.summary: Implicit classification through security assessment requirements. Services with public opinion attributes or social mobilization capacity face higher scrutiny (Art. 17). No formal tiering.

uk-ai-framework.summary: No centralized risk classification. Each sector regulator determines risk thresholds within their domain. Risk-proportionate principle applied contextually.

us-ai-policy.summary: No risk classification framework. EO 14179 revoked prior dual-use foundation model reporting requirements. Innovation-first approach avoids risk categorization.

canada-ai.summary: AIDA proposed high-impact AI classification but died in Parliament. Voluntary Code does not define risk tiers.

iso-42001.summary: Requires organizations to establish AI risk assessment processes (Clause 6.1) but does not prescribe fixed risk tiers. Organizations define their own criteria.

eu-ai-act.summary: Comprehensive: Art. 13 instructions for use, Art. 50 deepfake/AI content disclosure, Art. 85 right to explanation for high-risk decisions. Strongest transparency regime.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 5 (Transparency): disclose AI use, provide explanations of decisions, maintain audit trails. Principle-level without specific mechanisms.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP-1.5, GOVERN-1.5, MEASURE-2.8/2.9 address explainability. Detailed operational guidance for transparency implementation.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 3: meaningful transparency and responsible disclosure. Foundational — influenced most national frameworks.

singapore-maigf.summary: Operations Management principle includes explainability. Practical guidance on explaining AI decisions to affected individuals.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Principle 11: transparency and explainability as core values. Policy action area on transparency implementation.

china-genai.summary: Art. 12 mandates content labeling (watermarks/metadata) for generated content. Art. 9 requires disclosure of AI use. Focused on content identification rather than decision explainability.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Principle 2: appropriate transparency and explainability proportionate to risk. Applied through sector regulators with domain-specific interpretations.

us-ai-policy.summary: Section 2 calls for AI free from ideological bias. No transparency disclosure requirements. Prior transparency mandates under EO 14110 revoked.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary Code includes transparency as a principle. AIDA had proposed transparency obligations for high-impact systems but did not pass.

iso-42001.summary: Annex A controls A.4.3-A.4.5 address transparency, communication, and explainability. Requires documented transparency policies and processes.

eu-ai-act.summary: Clear provider/deployer obligations with designated responsibilities. Conformity assessment for high-risk. AI Office for GPAI oversight. Legal persons identified.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 6 (Accountability): organizations should be answerable for AI outcomes. No designated responsible officers or legal obligations.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: GOVERN function establishes organizational accountability structures. Roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths defined. No legal enforcement.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 5: AI actors should be accountable. Recommends mechanisms but leaves implementation to national frameworks.

singapore-maigf.summary: Internal governance structures recommended. Board/C-suite accountability for AI governance. Practical organizational models provided.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Accountability as a core value. Recommends liability frameworks and redress mechanisms. Non-binding.

china-genai.summary: Provider bears primary responsibility for generated content (Art. 9). Algorithm registration and security assessments mandatory (Art. 17). Multi-agency enforcement by CAC and six other agencies.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Principle 4: accountability and governance with clear oversight. Existing regulators enforce within their domains. No unified AI accountability body.

us-ai-policy.summary: Section 4 directs agencies to develop action plans. Accountability focused on federal agencies, not private sector. Minimal prescriptive requirements.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary Code includes accountability principle. AIDA had proposed criminal liability for reckless AI deployment causing harm. Only voluntary commitments remain.

iso-42001.summary: Clause 5.3 requires assignment of roles and responsibilities. Clauses 9.2-9.3 mandate internal audits and management reviews. Certifiable accountability framework.

eu-ai-act.summary: Complements GDPR. Art. 10 data governance for training data. Biometric data restrictions. Data protection impact assessments for high-risk AI.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 4 (Privacy and Security): protect personal data in AI systems. References PDPA (Act 709) but no AI-specific data requirements.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP and MEASURE address data privacy risks. References NIST Privacy Framework. No binding data protection requirements.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: References privacy as a value. Defers to national data protection laws for specific requirements.

singapore-maigf.summary: References PDPA compliance. Data management guidance for AI training data. AI Verify includes data governance testing.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Data protection as a core principle. Policy action area on data governance. Broad guidance without specific mechanisms.

china-genai.summary: Art. 7 requires lawful data sources with consent for personal information. Art. 11 prohibits unnecessary personal data collection. Art. 14 protects user input and usage records. Builds on PIPL.

uk-ai-framework.summary: ICO enforces data protection for AI under UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018. No AI-specific data provisions but strong existing regime.

us-ai-policy.summary: No AI-specific data privacy provisions. Section 2 policy statement does not address data protection. No federal comprehensive privacy law.

canada-ai.summary: CPPA (Part 1 of Bill C-27) also died in Parliament. Voluntary Code mentions data governance but has no binding data protection provisions for AI.

iso-42001.summary: Annex A control A.7.4 addresses data privacy in AI systems. A.8.4 covers data protection in operations. Integrated with organizational data management.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 10 bias monitoring in training data. Prohibited uses include social scoring and emotion recognition in certain contexts. Conformity assessment covers bias.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 3 (Fairness): prevent discrimination, ensure equitable outcomes. Principle 2 (Inclusiveness): diverse stakeholder representation.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP-2.3, MEASURE-2.6/2.10/2.11 address bias detection and mitigation. Companion NIST AI 100-4 provides bias management guidance.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 1: inclusive growth and sustainable development. Fairness embedded in values but no specific bias testing requirements.

singapore-maigf.summary: Fairness principle with practical guidance. AI Verify testing toolkit includes fairness metrics. Sector-specific fairness benchmarks.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Fairness and non-discrimination as core values. Strongest emphasis on equity among international frameworks.

china-genai.summary: Art. 4 prohibits discrimination. Art. 8 requires training data diversity and objectivity. Focus on preventing content that promotes ethnic or gender discrimination.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Principle 3: fairness — AI should not discriminate or create unfair outcomes. Equality Act 2010 applies. Sector regulators develop domain-specific fairness standards.

us-ai-policy.summary: Section 2 calls for AI free from ideological bias or engineered social agendas. No technical fairness testing requirements. Prior bias mitigation guidance revoked.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary Code includes fairness principle. No binding bias testing or fairness assessment requirements.

iso-42001.summary: Annex A control A.7.5 addresses bias in AI data. A.3.3 covers stakeholder impact assessment including fairness. Process-based rather than prescriptive metrics.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 14: mandatory human oversight for high-risk AI. Deployers must enable human intervention. Stop mechanisms required. Strongest human-in-the-loop mandate.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 7 (Reliability, Safety and Control): human oversight and intervention capability. Principle-level without specific mechanisms.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: GOVERN-1.4 and MANAGE functions address human oversight. Risk-proportionate human involvement recommended.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 4: robustness, security and safety — includes human oversight. Recommends human intervention capability.

singapore-maigf.summary: Human-in-the-loop recommended for high-impact decisions. Agentic AI addendum specifically addresses autonomous agent oversight.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Human oversight embedded in human rights approach. Recommends meaningful human control over AI systems.

china-genai.summary: Art. 9 requires providers to take measures to prevent harmful content generation. Art. 14 mandates human review and complaint handling. Content moderation implies human oversight.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Principle 4 (accountability) and Principle 5 (contestability) imply human oversight. No specific human-in-the-loop mandate. Left to sector regulators.

us-ai-policy.summary: No human oversight requirements. Prior human oversight mandates under EO 14110 revoked. Focus on removing regulatory barriers.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary Code includes human oversight principle. AIDA had proposed human oversight requirements for high-impact systems but did not pass.

iso-42001.summary: Annex A control A.3.2 addresses human oversight roles and responsibilities. Requires defined human involvement processes but does not mandate specific oversight levels.

eu-ai-act.summary: National competent authorities + AI Office for GPAI. Market surveillance. Fines up to 35M EUR / 7% global turnover. Product bans for non-compliance.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: No enforcement mechanism. Voluntary guidelines with no penalties. Future legislation possible but not currently planned.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: No direct enforcement. Market incentive through adoption. Referenced in some federal procurement requirements.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Peer review among member countries. Moral authority. Influences national legislation but no direct enforcement.

singapore-maigf.summary: AI Verify testing framework provides soft incentive. PDPC may enforce data protection aspects. No AI-specific penalties.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Readiness Assessment Methodology for self-assessment. No enforcement. Relies on member state implementation.

china-genai.summary: Multi-agency enforcement led by CAC. Algorithm registration mandatory. Security assessments required. Penalties follow cybersecurity and data security laws — warnings, fines, service suspension, criminal liability.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Existing sector regulators enforce within their domains using existing legal powers. No AI-specific fines. DRCF coordinates across regulators. Non-statutory initially.

us-ai-policy.summary: No AI-specific enforcement mechanism. EO directs federal agencies to develop action plans. No penalties for private sector. State-level AI laws emerging independently.

canada-ai.summary: No enforcement mechanism. AIDA had proposed AI and Data Commissioner with enforcement powers but died. Voluntary Code has no penalties.

iso-42001.summary: Third-party certification audits provide market incentive. No regulatory enforcement power. Certification can be withdrawn for non-conformity.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 9 mandates risk management systems for high-risk AI. Continuous, iterative process throughout AI lifecycle. Most prescriptive binding risk management requirement.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 7 addresses reliability and safety. Encourages risk management but no specific risk management system requirements.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Core purpose is AI risk management. Four functions (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage) provide comprehensive voluntary risk management framework. Gold standard for operational guidance.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 4 recommends systematic risk management. Foundational influence but no operational detail.

singapore-maigf.summary: Risk management embedded in Internal Governance and Operations Management principles. Practical, self-assessment approach.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Proportionality principle (Principle 5) implies risk assessment. Ethical Impact Assessment policy area addresses risk. High-level guidance.

china-genai.summary: Art. 17 requires security assessments and algorithm registration for high-risk services. Risk management focused on content safety and social stability rather than general AI risk.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Principle 1: safety, security and robustness with continuous risk management. Sector regulators develop domain-specific risk frameworks.

us-ai-policy.summary: No risk management requirements. Section 4 action plan covers national security risks broadly. Revoked prior risk assessment mandates for dual-use models.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary Code recommends safety measures. AIDA had proposed mandatory risk mitigation for high-impact systems. No binding risk management framework.

iso-42001.summary: Clause 6.1 mandates AI risk assessment integrated into the management system. Annex A provides risk treatment controls. Certifiable risk management process.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 57-58 regulatory sandboxes. Real-world testing provisions. SME support measures. Balanced with compliance burden for high-risk AI.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Voluntary approach designed to encourage innovation. No compliance burden. Aligned with Malaysia's digital economy ambitions.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Designed to be innovation-friendly. Voluntary, flexible framework. Supports responsible innovation without creating compliance barriers.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 6-8 specifically support AI R&D investment, digital ecosystems, and enabling policy environments. Strong innovation emphasis.

singapore-maigf.summary: AI Verify sandbox for testing. Innovation-friendly governance. Government investment in AI research and industry adoption.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Education, research, and development policy areas support innovation. Balances innovation with ethical considerations.

china-genai.summary: Art. 3 encourages AI innovation and international competitiveness. Regulatory approach balances innovation support with content control and social stability requirements.

uk-ai-framework.summary: Explicitly pro-innovation approach. Regulatory sandboxes encouraged. Avoids creating new regulatory bodies. Designed to maintain UK competitiveness.

us-ai-policy.summary: Primary focus is removing barriers to AI innovation. Strongest innovation-first posture globally. Revoked prior safety requirements seen as innovation barriers.

canada-ai.summary: Voluntary approach avoids compliance burden. Government invests in AI through Pan-Canadian AI Strategy. Light-touch regulation intended to foster innovation.

iso-42001.summary: Certification provides market advantage and trust signal. Management system approach can streamline AI governance. Does not directly promote or hinder innovation.
